proposition: Leading articulation of the totality-of-circumstances physical-control test, approving a non-exhaustive jury-instruction factor list including key location, vehicle state, driver position, awake/asleep, headlights, weather, etc. Touchstone is whether the defendant posed a present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion.
brief_block: The leading articulation is the Arizona Supreme Court's decision in State v. Zaragoza, 221 Ariz. 49, 209 P.3d 629 (2009), which approved a non-exhaustive jury instruction listing factors including "whether the vehicle was running or the ignition was on; where the key was located; where and in what position the driver was found in the vehicle; whether the person was awake or asleep; if the vehicle's headlights were on; where the vehicle was stopped (in the road or legally parked); whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road; time of day and weather conditions; if the heater or air conditioner was on; whether the windows were up or down; and any explanation of the circumstances advanced by the defense." Id. at 53. The touchstone is whether the defendant posed "a present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion."
opinion_block: The Court listed several factors to be considered in determining whether a defendant exercised actual physical control, including [the factor list quoted in the brief]. Id. (citing Atkinson v. State, 331 Md. 199, 627 A.2d 1019, 1027 (1993)). We further noted that this list is not exhaustive. [Zaragoza ¶ 12, quoting State v. Love, 182 Ariz. 324, 326, 897 P.2d 626, 628 (1995).] . . . the instruction here closely tracks Love's conclusion that a person is in actual physical control when, under the totality of the facts, the person "posed a threat to the public by the exercise of present or imminent control" over a vehicle "while impaired." [Zaragoza ¶ 17.] . . . It is up to you to examine all the available evidence and weigh its credibility in determining whether the defendant actually posed a threat to the public by the exercise of present or imminent control of the vehicle while impaired. [Zaragoza ¶ 21, recommended jury instruction.] . . . whether the defendant's current or imminent control of the vehicle presented a real danger to [himself] [herself] or others at the time alleged. [Zaragoza ¶ 21.]
finding_analysis:
The 11-factor list is verbatim against Zaragoza ¶ 12 (with one trivial OCR variant), but a hidden citation point: Zaragoza did not author this list — it quotes Love (182 Ariz. 324 (1995)) verbatim, and Love in turn cited Atkinson v. State (Md. 1993). The memo treats the factor list as Zaragoza's articulation, when it is more accurately Zaragoza re-quoting Love.

The second quote — "a present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion" — is FABRICATED. That exact phrase does not appear anywhere in Zaragoza. The court's actual touchstone language is materially different: (1) "present or imminent control" of the vehicle (¶¶ 17, 21), (2) "posed a threat to the public" / "presented a real danger to himself or others" (¶¶ 17, 21), and (3) "actually posed a threat to the public by the exercise of present or imminent control over [the vehicle] while impaired" (¶ 21). The memo's phrase appears to be a stitched paraphrase that substitutes "risk of putting the vehicle in motion" for the court's "control" / "threat to the public" / "real danger" language.

The substantive distinction matters. Zaragoza's touchstone is about present or imminent CONTROL — which the court treats as broader than imminent driving. The court explicitly rejected the lower court's proposal that the test should focus on the defendant's "ultimate purpose of placing the vehicle in motion" (¶ 20), holding that defendant's intent is not an element and that any instruction requiring the jury to consider the defendant's purpose "incorrectly states the law." The memo's paraphrase ("risk of putting the vehicle in motion") leans toward the very motion-focused framing Zaragoza rejected.

Support: the proposition that Zaragoza is a leading totality-of-circumstances articulation that approves a non-exhaustive multifactor jury instruction is fully supported. The proposition that the touchstone is "present or imminent risk of putting the vehicle in motion" is not — the actual touchstone is present or imminent CONTROL posing a threat or real danger to the public. Because the misstatement of the touchstone slightly extends Zaragoza toward a motion-focused test it explicitly declined to adopt, classification is Partially supported. With the FABRICATED quote, color is Yellow with badge "Paraphrase presented as direct quote".

proposition: Florida totality approach: defendant must be in or on the vehicle and have the capability to operate it, evaluated under the totality of circumstances.
brief_block: Florida is the other leading exemplar; its courts ask whether the defendant was "physically in or on the vehicle" and had "the capability to operate" it, evaluated under the totality of circumstances. Florida Std. Jury Instr. (Crim.) 28.1; State v. Fitzgerald, 63 So. 3d 75 (Fla. 2d DCA 2011).
opinion_block: To prove this element, "the defendant must be physically in or on the vehicle and have the capability to operate the vehicle, regardless of whether [he][she] is actually operating the vehicle at the time." Fla. Std. Jury Instr. (Crim.) 28.1. . . . In analyzing the necessary proof of this element, the First District reviewed cases from Alabama, where the courts review the totality of the circumstances to determine whether someone is in actual physical control. Jones v. State, 510 So.2d 1147, 1148 (Fla. 1st DCA 1987). [Fitzgerald, 63 So.3d at 77.]
finding_analysis:
Both quoted phrases are verbatim, but they are quoted from the Florida Standard Jury Instruction (Crim.) 28.1, not from Fitzgerald's own holding — Fitzgerald reproduces the jury-instruction language. The memo accurately credits the jury instruction in its parallel signal but presents Fitzgerald as the leading totality-of-circumstances case.

The substantive issue: Fitzgerald does NOT itself articulate or apply a "totality of the circumstances" framework. The phrase "totality of the circumstances" appears in Fitzgerald only once, and only to describe Alabama's approach (via Jones v. State citing Cagle v. City of Gadsden). Fitzgerald's own analysis is more straightforwardly a sufficiency-of-evidence ruling: defendant in driver's seat + keys readily produced from her hand + car stopped in intersection with lights on = prima facie case under the jury instruction's "physically in . . . and capability to operate" language. There is no enumerated factor list and no totality framework adopted as Florida's test in the opinion.

The memo's framing thus overstates Fitzgerald: it (a) attributes language to the case that the case itself attributes to the Florida Standard Jury Instruction, and (b) presents the case as embracing a "totality of circumstances" test when the case mentions that phrase only descriptively about Alabama law. The jury instruction quote and the basic proposition (Florida requires physical presence in the vehicle plus capability to operate) are supported; the totality-of-circumstances overlay is overstated relative to what Fitzgerald itself says. Partially supported.

proposition: Foundational Florida totality-of-circumstances articulation widely treated as the source of the camp-2 multifactor line.
brief_block: Fieselman v. State, 537 So. 2d 603 (Fla. 1988) [MEDIUM — verified via analyze_citations against CourtListener (cluster 1130447); full opinion not retrieved], is widely treated as the foundational Florida decision in the totality-of-circumstances line.
opinion_block: [Caption:] District Court of Appeal of Florida, Third District. November 22, 1988. . . . [Holding:] We believe that such an inference can be drawn since a person who has placed keys in the ignition of a vehicle may be as much in actual physical control of the vehicle as a person seated behind the wheel of the vehicle. . . . evidence that the key was in the ignition does not inexorably lead to the conclusion that the defendant was in actual physical control of the vehicle. It is merely a fact — along with the defendant's presence asleep and intoxicated in the vehicle — which, being capable of establishing the defendant's actual physical control of the vehicle, precludes the conclusion that as a matter of law the defendant was not in actual physical control of the vehicle and thus precludes the entry of a dismissal of the charges.
finding_analysis:
Two distinct problems.

First, the citation is wrong. The memo cites Fieselman as "537 So. 2d 603 (Fla. 1988)" — implying a Florida Supreme Court decision. Fieselman is in fact a Fla. 3d DCA decision (District Court of Appeal of Florida, Third District, November 22, 1988). The correct cite is "537 So. 2d 603 (Fla. 3d DCA 1988)." This matters because the memo's framing of Fieselman as "foundational" / leading authority would carry very different weight as a Florida Supreme Court decision vs. an intermediate appellate decision from one of five DCAs.

Second, and more importantly, Fieselman does not articulate a totality-of-circumstances test, does not enumerate any multifactor list, and is not in fact "the foundational Florida decision in the totality-of-circumstances line." Fieselman is a sufficiency / motion-to-dismiss ruling on a narrow question: whether the presence of keys in the ignition (combined with defendant lying down asleep in the front seat of a parked car in a parking lot, engine cold) sufficed to defeat a motion to dismiss. The opinion concludes that keys-in-ignition + presence-asleep-while-intoxicated is enough to take the case to the factfinder. Nothing in the opinion announces a totality test or a multifactor framework. The phrase "totality of the circumstances" does not appear in Fieselman. The opinion does not purport to set a general framework for Florida's physical-control jurisprudence; it is a case-specific certiorari denial.

The memo's parenthetical concedes the system did not retrieve the full opinion ("verified via analyze_citations against CourtListener (cluster 1130447); full opinion not retrieved"). On the actual opinion text, the proposition is not supported. Color is Red.

proposition: Maryland's leading articulation of the multifactor totality test; Zaragoza adopts its factor list verbatim.
brief_block: The Maryland Court of Appeals' decision in Atkinson v. State, 331 Md. 199, 627 A.2d 1019 (1993) [MEDIUM — verified against CourtListener (cluster 2337244); full opinion not retrieved; relied on as represented in Zaragoza's genealogy], is the most-cited articulation of the Zaragoza-style factor list and is treated by most commentators as the modern source of that approach rather than a Camp 4 outlier. Maryland's current articulation resembles the totality approach in Zaragoza.
opinion_block: [Atkinson factor list, 331 Md. at 216, 627 A.2d at 1027:] 1) whether or not the vehicle's engine is running, or the ignition on; 2) where and in what position the person is found in the vehicle; 3) whether the person is awake or asleep; 4) where the vehicle's ignition key is located; 5) whether the vehicle's headlights are on; 6) whether the vehicle is located in the roadway or is legally parked. . . . No one factor alone will necessarily be dispositive of whether the defendant was in "actual physical control" of the vehicle. Rather, each must be considered with an eye towards whether there is in fact present or imminent exercise of control over the vehicle or, instead, whether the vehicle is merely being used as a stationary shelter.
finding_analysis:
The first half of the proposition is supported: Atkinson IS Maryland's leading articulation of the multifactor totality test for "actual physical control" under Md. Transp. § 21-902 / § 11-114, and the Court of Appeals (then Maryland's highest court) enumerates a six-factor non-exhaustive list at 331 Md. at 216 and frames the inquiry around "present or imminent exercise of control" versus "stationary shelter." The memo's broader framing of Atkinson as the modern source of the camp-2 approach is defensible: Atkinson's framing is regularly cited by other state courts (including indirectly through Love in Zaragoza).

The second half — that "Zaragoza adopts [Atkinson's] factor list verbatim" — is not supported. Two distinct problems:

(1) Citation chain. Zaragoza did not adopt the list directly from Atkinson; Zaragoza quoted the list from its own prior decision in State v. Love, 182 Ariz. 324, 326 (1995). Love had earlier cited Atkinson when crafting its list. The memo's phrasing skips the Love step entirely.

(2) Not verbatim. Atkinson lists SIX factors: engine running/ignition on; position; awake/asleep; key location; headlights; roadway vs. legally parked. The list quoted in Zaragoza (from Love) contains ELEVEN-PLUS factors and adds several Atkinson does not have: whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road; time of day; weather conditions; whether the heater or air conditioner was on; whether the windows were up or down; and any explanation advanced by the defense. Love expanded Atkinson's list substantially. Even the overlapping items are rephrased (e.g., Atkinson's "where the vehicle is located in the roadway or is legally parked" appears in Love/Zaragoza as "where the vehicle was stopped (in the road or legally parked); whether the driver had voluntarily pulled off the road" — two factors, not one). "Adopts . . . verbatim" is therefore inaccurate; the more accurate framing would be "derives from and expands" or "builds on."

Because the genealogy claim is overstated and the "verbatim" claim is specifically false, classification is Partially supported. The core characterization of Atkinson as Maryland's leading totality articulation holds; the lineage detail does not.
